title-group.article-title: Success factors in prevention activities and training intervention against workplace violence in healthcare: A systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Health Sciences, Magna Graecia University of Catanzaro, Catanzaro, Italy
bDepartment of Biomedical and Dental Sciences and Morphofunctional Imaging, University of Messina, Messina, Italy
cDepartment of of Social Psychology, Department of Philosophy, Sociology, Pedagogy, Applied Psychology, University of Padua, Padua, Italy
dDepartment of Clinical and Experimental Medicine, University of Messina, Messina, Italy

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Department of Health Sciences, Magna Graecia University of Catanzaro, Catanzaro, Italy. chiaralabarbiera@gmail.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: Workplace violence (WPV) against healthcare workers (HCWs) represents a significant occupational hazard and is a widespread issue globally. WPV is linked to decreased job satisfaction, commitment, and efficiency among healthcare workers, leading to a poorer quality of life. This review aims to identify and evaluate effective strategies for the prevention and intervention against workplace violence in the healthcare sector.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: Systematic review.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines. A comprehensive literature search was carried out in Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus databases, focusing on studies published in the last four years. The selection process followed predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria based on the PICOS framework.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Out of 442 initially identified studies, eight met the inclusion criteria and were included in the review. The findings indicate that training programs incorporating simulations and behavioral economics principles significantly improve healthcare workers' ability to manage aggression. The use of validated assessment tools to identify risks of violence and the implementation of effective organizational policies have proven essential in reducing the incidence of aggression, thus enhancing the safety and capability of staff.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: An integrated approach combining advanced training, organizational support, and preventive policies is essential to foster a safer work environment and protect HCWs' well-being. Additional research is necessary to reinforce existing evidence and explore innovative interventions.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Workplace violence (WPV) against healthcare workers (HCWs) represents a significant occupational hazard and is a widespread issue globally, leading to serious consequences for victims, patients, and healthcare organizations [1]. WPV has been linked to decreased job satisfaction, commitment, and efficiency among healthcare workers. It also leads to poorer quality of life, heightened stress, disrupted sleep and in severe cases, death [2,3]. Furthermore, WPV negatively affects the retention of healthcare staff and diminishes the quality of medical care provided [4].

The Covid-19 emergency has significantly exacerbated these issues. During the pandemic, there was a marked increase in incidents of violence: numerous healthcare workers were subjected to assaults by patients and their families, exposing them to serious risks [5,6]. This escalation in aggressive episodes intensified stress levels, decreased emotional wellness, heightened irritability, and triggered symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder [7]. Healthcare workers in hospital emergency departments (EDs) are particularly at risk of violence due to their unique work environment, where violent and mentally ill patients require care and may need restraints for their safety and that of the HCWs [8]. The combination of a high-stress environment and the increased incidence of violence during the pandemic has further highlighted the urgent need for effective WPV prevention and intervention strategies.

Existing research highlights that healthcare services are inherently stressful environments, where the perception of threat can trigger aggressive responses from patients [9]. This aggression deteriorates the quality of life for healthcare professionals and adversely impacts their mental health, leading to emotional exhaustion, suicidal thoughts, depression, and anxiety [[10], [11], [12]]. These psychological and physical consequences lead to job dissatisfaction and decreased productivity, ultimately contributing to burnout, a psychological syndrome resulting from prolonged exposure to chronic interpersonal stressors at work. Burnout is characterized by three core dimensions: overwhelming exhaustion, cynicism and detachment from the job, and a sense of ineffectiveness and lack of achievement [13]. WPV affects both the well-being of healthcare workers and the overall efficiency of healthcare systems. It leads to reduced care quality, increased absenteeism, and lower productivity. A major challenge is the frequent under-reporting of incidents in emergency departments, which limits accurate monitoring and hinders the development of effective prevention strategies [14]. To address workplace violence effectively, it is essential to adopt preventive strategies that reduce incidents and improve staff preparedness, fostering safer environments. Studies highlight the effectiveness of well-structured interventions, although evidence remains limited. Future research should focus on environmental and organizational measures with high methodological quality. Strategies for addressing workplace violence can be broadly classified into three main categories, each operating at different organizational and clinical levels. The first category, prevention, includes interventions aimed at reducing the likelihood of violent incidents before they occur. These strategies typically involve structured staff training on de-escalation and communication skills, modifying environmental and organizational conditions (e.g., improving signage, layout, and visibility), and implementing clear, safety-oriented policies and reporting procedures. The second category, protection, focuses on minimizing harm when violent or potentially violent situations arise. This involves the use of safety devices (such as alarms or secure rooms), the establishment of rapid response teams, and the adoption of emergency protocols designed to ensure staff safety and coordinate immediate support. Finally, treatment refers to measures implemented after an incident has occurred, including psychological support for affected workers, post-event debriefing sessions, and structured follow-up aimed at promoting recovery and reducing long-term consequences. Together, these three domains provide a comprehensive framework for managing and mitigating workplace violence in healthcare settings [15].

From a theoretical perspective, the importance of prevention and intervention strategies to address workplace violence can be understood through the Self-Determination Theory (SDT) proposed by Deci and Ryan. According to SDT, motivation, psychological well-being, and adaptive behaviors increase when the basic psychological needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness are fulfilled [16]. In healthcare settings, interventions that strengthen workers’ sense of competence, autonomy, and relatedness can reduce stress, enhance resilience, and lower the likelihood of maladaptive responses in violent or high-risk situations. Fricke et al. [17] found that while strategies like training, debriefing, rapid response teams, and environmental changes show promise, the evidence remains limited. Nonetheless, strong leadership promoting inclusion, support, and respect is key to successful violence prevention.

The aim of this systematic review is to identify and evaluate effective strategies for the prevention and intervention against workplace violence in the healthcare sector. Through an in-depth analysis of the available evidence, this review aims to provide recommendations that can guide the implementation of effective preventive and intervention measures. This review emphasizes the need for further research to consolidate current knowledge and develop innovative new solutions.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This research was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines. Furthermore, the main question is framed according to the PICOS-guided eligibility criteria (Population/Intervention/Comparison/Outcome/Study design). The study protocol was registered on PROSPERO, the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews, National Institute of Health Research, University of York, with the registration number “CRD420251004469” available from https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/PROSPERO/view/CRD420251004469.

sec.title: Literature search strategy
sec.p: The literature search was conducted in the electronic databases Web of Science (WoS), PubMed and Scopus on November 15, 2024. The search was limited to studies published within the last 4 years (2020–2024 and restricted to English-language publications. This timeframe was intentionally selected to ensure the inclusion of the most recent and relevant evidence, considering the rapidly evolving nature of workplace violence prevention strategies and the substantial changes in healthcare settings following the COVID-19 pandemic. The following keywords were used to identify relevant articles: (“healthcare workers”) AND (“workplace violence” OR “workplace aggression”) AND (“prevention” AND “intervention”).

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: The screening and selection of studies was based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria according to the PICOS framework as follows: (P) population (healthcare workers of any type: physicians, nurses, others), (I) intervention (training programs focused on de-escalation techniques, self-defense training and realistic simulation exercises, interventions to improve the organization's safety policies and environmental changes to reduce workplace violence.), (C) comparison (none), (O) outcomes (the prevalence of various types of workplace violence in hospital settings and the effectiveness of prevention and intervention strategies), and (S) study design (Experimental studies and observational studies).

In addition to these inclusion criteria, several exclusion criteria were applied to ensure the relevance and methodological consistency of the selected studies. We excluded studies involving workers from non-healthcare settings, as well as studies examining forms of violence unrelated to workplace dynamics (e.g., domestic violence or interpersonal conflicts between colleagues). Interventions not directly related to workplace violence were also excluded. Studies focusing solely on questionnaires that did not assess workplace violence risk or the effectiveness of prevention or intervention strategies were not considered. Furthermore, case studies, reviews, meta-analyses, opinion articles, editorials, commentaries, theses, and dissertations were excluded. Table 1 provides an overview of the eligibility criteria.Table 1
Eligibility criteria for the screening and selection of studies.Table 1
CriteriaInclusionExclusion
PopulationHealthcare workers (physicians, nurses, others) in healthcare setting (e.g. hospital, healthcare facilities, inpatient units)Other type of workers
ExposureViolence and assault by patients and their relatives or companionsViolence due to personal relationships, violence between workers
InterventionTraining programs focusing on de-escalation techniques, self-defense training, and realistic simulation exercises. Interventions designed to improve organizational safety policies and environmental modifications to reduce workplace violence. Studies evaluating the effectiveness and validation of questionnaires designed to assess workplace violence risksInterventions not directly related to workplace violence, such as general staff training or personal development programs unrelated to safety. Studies focusing on questionnaires unrelated to assessing risks or effectiveness of workplace violence prevention measures
OutcomeIncreasing the skills of health care workers and their well-being and safetyOther types of results
Study designExperimental studies (e.g. randomised and non randomised controlled trials, quasi-experimental studies); observational studies (e.g. cohort studies, cross-sectional studies, longitudinal studies)Case studies, reviews, meta-analyses
Publication typeResearch articlesOpinion articles, editorials, commentaries, theses and dissertations

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: All articles identified through the literature search were transferred to the Zotero literature management program, and duplicates were removed. Two reviewers independently screened the titles and abstracts based on the predefined eligibility criteria. Full-text articles of potentially relevant studies were then assessed independently by the same two reviewers. Any disagreements at either stage were resolved through discussion and, when necessary, consultation with a third reviewer. Studies that met all criteria were included in the final review.

sec.title: Data collection process
sec.p: The authors conducted an independent and rigorous review of the study results. Two reviewers independently screened the titles and abstracts of all retrieved articles based on the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Full-text articles of potentially relevant studies were then assessed independently by the same two reviewers. Disagreements at any stage were resolved through discussion or, when necessary, by consulting a third reviewer. The methodological quality and risk of bias of the included studies were assessed using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) Critical Appraisal Tools, selecting the checklist appropriate for each study design. The appraisal was conducted independently by the two reviewers following the same consensus procedure. The use of JBI ensured a standardized and transparent evaluation across heterogeneous designs. No studies were excluded based on quality appraisal; instead, the findings informed the interpretation of evidence in the narrative synthesis.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
A total of 442 studies were published from 2020 to 2024. A total of 40 studies were excluded before screening because of duplication. The remaining 402 records were screened, and 69 records were excluded because they were opinion articles, book chapters, case reports, letters to the editor. 333 titles/abstracts were screened and subsequently, 31 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility. Eight studies were included in the review (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
PRISMA 2020 flow diagram for systematic review.Fig. 1

The main characteristics of the included studies were analysed descriptively and summarised in Table 2. Four studies highlighted the use of interactive training sessions, each incorporating elements of de-escalation techniques and realistic simulation exercises.Table 2
Characteristics of the studies included in the review.Table 2
Authors and YearDesign of the StudyAimsSampleInterventionResults
Gupta et al., 2023Pre–post quasi-experimental intervention (longitudinal follow-up: immediate, 3, 6, 12 months)To investigate the prevalence of WPV experienced by hospitalists and evaluate the effects of de-escalation training on their ability to manage patient aggression37 hospitalists from a community academic urban medical center in Baltimore,A de-escalation training intervention was conducted, comprising a 3-h session with theoretical lessons on workplace violence and practical role-play exercises, including self-defense. The “Confidence in Coping with Patient Aggression” questionnaire was administered before and after the training to assess the impact on participants' confidence in managing aggressive behaviorsHigh prevalence of WPV reported, with 86.5 % of participants experiencing some form of violence, predominantly verbal abuse. De-escalation training significantly increased hospitalists' confidence in coping with aggressive patients, with sustained improvement over time
Brunero et al., 2021Cross-sectional studyTo evaluate the utility of the violence prevention climate scale by generalist healthcare professionals194 healthcare staff from various departments including the emergency department and medical/surgical wards at a major Australian tertiary referral hospital“Violence Prevention Climate scale” was administered. A content analysis of an open-ended question on violence prevention management strategies was also conductedThe study found the 9-item staff factor scale of the violence prevention climate scale to be applicable in the general hospital setting, identifying several categories of violence prevention and management strategies recognized by staff
Hatta et al., 2023Methodological validation study (content validity, face validity, pilot testing)To develop and validate an instrument to assess workplace violence risk factors in healthcare settingsIncludes healthcare administrators, workers, and clientsThree questionnaires were developed to get the responses from healthcare administrators, workers, and clients, representing the three components of Questionnaires to Assess Workplace Violence Risk Factors (QAWRF)The developed tool has good content, face validity, and reliability, indicating its utility in assessing workplace violence risk factors effectively
D'Ettorre et al., 2020Methodological development and validation questionnaire (Delphi method + pilot test)To develop and validate a questionnaire for preliminary risk assessment of type II workplace violence in emergency departmentsHealthcare workers in emergency departments, including physicians and nurses, within the studied hospitalDevelopment of a questionnaire called “Emergency Department Workplace Violence Questionnaire” (ED WPV-Q), to assess the risk of type II workplace violence (WPV) in hospital emergency departmentsThe developed “Emergency Department Workplace Violence-Questionnaire” (ED WPV-Q) identified various risk factors and provided a medium risk level of workplace violence for healthcare workers. The questionnaire facilitated the identification of specific risk areas and suggested organizational and environmental interventions
Vrablik et al., 2020Qualitative grounded theory study (interviews and focus groups across multiple ED sites)To explore health worker experiences with patient assaults in emergency departments and to develop interventionsHealthcare workers across five different emergency department sites in two geographic regionsConducted a qualitative study using grounded theory to analyze experiences of ED staff managing agitation and assaults across five sites, aiming to create a preventative framework for workplace violence against ED health workersThe study identified interventions across micro, meso, and macro levels of the healthcare system and emphasized the need for comprehensive approaches at multiple phases of emergency preparedness to effectively enhance workplace safety
Brunero et al., 2021Sequential explanatory mixed-methods (pre–post quantitative + focus group qualitative)To develop and evaluate the effectiveness of tabletop exercises for training health practitioners in violence prevention and managementHealth practitionersThe study comprised of two phases 1) quasi–experimental without control and 2) focus group to evaluate the effectiveness of the VPM tabletop exerciseThe study found significant improvements in healthcare professionals' confidence levels regarding their ability to manage violent incidents following the tabletop exercises. Additionally, participants appreciated the role clarity, adult learning principles, and organizational support provided during the exercises
Liu et al., 2022Quasi-experimental single-blind study with three randomized groups (Control, SBME, SBME + BE)To examine the effectiveness of SBME and SBME combined with BE in improving medical residents' capabilities in coping with workplace violence190 medical residents from Harbin Medical University190 participants were randomised into three study groups to respectively test the effect of simulation-based medical education only and simulation-based medical education plus behavioural economics interventions, compared with a control groupThe study found that both SBME and SBME combined with BE significantly enhanced participants' perception, attitudes, and self-efficacy related to workplace violence compared to a control group
Sharifi et al., 2020Quasi-experimental before–after studyTo evaluate the effects of an education program, a risk assessment checklist, and a preventive protocol on the incidence of violence against nurses in an emergency departmentNurses working in the emergency department of Tohid Hospital, SanandajParticipants attended a 4-h workshop, where they were trained on using a risk assessment checklist and preventive protocol for violence-prone persons. This checklist was integrated into patient medical records in the emergency department to assess and manage the risk of violence at admission. The intervention included training sessions and daily support from the researcher to ensure correct implementationSignificant reduction in the mean score of violence from 8.4 pre-intervention to 2.7 post-intervention. There were also notable decreases in verbal abuse and other forms of violence post-intervention

Gupta et al. [18] conducted a study at an urban medical center in Baltimore involving hospitalists who underwent mandatory de-escalation training. The program included 3 h of instruction, role-play focused on self-defense, and discussion of the five-phase assault cycle. Surveys administered before, immediately after, and at 3, 6, and 12 months post-training assessed changes in participants’ confidence in managing patient aggression and the sustainability of training effects. The study findings revealed that in the 12 months prior to the de-escalation training, 86.5 % of the participants reported experiencing at least one form of workplace violence, including verbal abuse (83.8 %), racial abuse (29.7 %), physical violence (18.9 %), and sexual abuse (16.2 %). Following the training, there was a significant increase in the average confidence scores, from a pre-training level of 43.2–68.5 immediately after the training. The increased confidence levels were sustained over time, remaining significantly elevated at three months (57.2), six months (60.2), and twelve months (59.9) (all p < 0.05 and ptrend <0.05).

Brunero et al. [19] evaluated Violence Prevention Management (VPM) tabletop exercises in a tertiary hospital using a mixed-method design. Conducted in departments at high risk for violence, the simulations were based on real incidents and facilitated by clinical educators. The study found that these sessions improved participants' understanding of their roles during ‘code black’ events and increased their confidence in managing violent situations. The integration of qualitative feedback reinforced the educational value of the exercises, highlighting their role in promoting preparedness, teamwork, and adherence to the hospital's zero-tolerance policy.

Liu et al. [20] conducted a quasi-experimental study to evaluate interventions for preventing workplace violence among medical residents. Using the Haddon matrix framework, the interventions addressed epidemiological and temporal factors to improve residents' ability to anticipate, manage, and respond to WPV before, during, and after incidents. Three groups participated in the study:1.
A control group that received traditional WPV prevention training.2.
A Simulation-Based Medical Education (SBME) group that underwent more intensive training using practical simulations to enhance real-life application skills.3.
A SBME + Behavioral Economics (BE) group that combined simulation training with behavioral economics principles to deepen understanding of patient interactions and decision-making processes that can prevent WPV.

This study demonstrated that training combining SBME with BE significantly enhances medical residents' abilities to manage workplace violence compared to traditional methods (p < 0.01). The inclusion of BE principles such as loss aversion improved the effectiveness of WPV management by providing insights into the psychological triggers of violence and enhancing doctor-patient communication. This approach not only increases residents' capacity to handle WPV but also promotes better health outcomes. The findings advocate for the broader implementation of this multidimensional training approach, which integrates behavioral economics into WPV prevention strategies, as a valuable tool in healthcare settings.

Sharifi et al. [21] conducted a quasi-experimental before-and-after study in the emergency department of Tohid Hospital, Iran, involving 37 nurses. A 4-h seminar held over two days introduced a risk assessment checklist and a preventive protocol for managing violent patients. The checklist evaluated six indicators—confusion, irritability, verbal and physical threats, object attacks, and yelling—and materials remained accessible for six weeks. The researcher ensured proper implementation through daily visits. Participants were mostly female (69.9 %), held a nursing degree (86.5 %), worked in inpatient care (73 %), and had rotating shifts (51.4 %). Prior to the intervention, 67.6 % reported the absence of reporting guidelines, and 73 % lacked violence management training. Post-intervention results showed significant reductions in violence. The average SOAS-R score dropped from 8.4 to 2.7 (p < 0.001); perceived WPV risk fell from 46 % to 35.1 % (p < 0.006); average incidents per nurse declined from 2.7 to 0.4 (p < 0.001). Physical violence decreased from 10.8 % to 5.4 %, and verbal abuse dropped from 89.2 % to 45.9 %, with the average number of episodes per nurse falling from 2.9 to 0.8 (p < 0.001). No cases of sexual violence were reported before or after, and racial violence decreased from 13.5 % to 5.4 %, though without statistical significance. Overall, the intervention significantly improved nurse safety and reduced WPV severity in the emergency department.
Four studies focus on tools for preventing workplace violence, specifically examining the validation of questionnaires designed to prevent and mitigate such incidents.

A study by Brunero et al. [22] utilized a cross-sectional de-identified survey method to evaluate the effectiveness of the “Violence Prevention Climate” scale [23] in non-psychiatric but high-risk clinical settings. The departments examined included the emergency department, medical/surgical wards, respiratory/infectious diseases, spinal care, renal units, correctional health, rehabilitation, and community services in a 450-bed tertiary hospital in Sydney. Content analysis of open-ended responses revealed key needs: increased staffing, improved nurse-to-patient ratios, greater security presence, better communication, use of de-escalation techniques, enhanced training, and safe spaces for violent patients. Comparative analyses across four clinical specialties (respiratory, emergency, spinal, others) showed significant intergroup differences (χ2 = 32.09, df = 3, p < 0.001). Post hoc Dunn tests with Bonferroni correction revealed significant differences between emergency and spinal departments (p < 0.001), and between emergency and other departments (p < 0.05), but not between emergency and respiratory departments (p = 0.08). The study supports the Violence Prevention Climate scale as a valid tool to assess the effectiveness of violence prevention strategies in general hospitals. It promotes a multidisciplinary safety approach and helps identify key areas for targeted intervention.

Hatta et al. [24] developed and validated the QAWRF, a reliable set of self-administered questionnaires to assess WPV risk factors in healthcare from the perspectives of administrators, workers, and clients. Tailored to each group's experience, the tool reduces bias and captures diverse perceptions of WPV. Using a quantitative risk assessment approach, respondents rated observed risk factors, addressing the unpredictable nature of WPV incidents. All domains within the QAWRF-administrator displayed acceptable Cronbach's alpha values ranging from 0.809 to 0.930. The QAWRF-worker also showed satisfactory Cronbach's alpha values ranging from 0.892 to 0.949. The QAWRF-client's worker domain, after item removal, improved to a Cronbach's alpha of 0.866, while the workplace domain scored 0.770, both acceptable. The QAWRF tool received excellent content validation, confirming its effectiveness in measuring WPV risk factors. Non-relevant items were removed to improve clarity and target-group specificity, and Likert scale descriptors were revised for better usability. QAWRF emerges as a valid, reliable, and practical tool for designing focused, resource-efficient WPV interventions in healthcare.

A study by d’Ettorre and colleagues [25] (2020) focused on developing, validating, and determining the reliability of the “Emergency Department Workplace Violence Questionnaire” (ED WPV-Q), a tool specifically designed to objectively assess the risk of Type II workplace violence (WPV) in hospital emergency departments. Type II WPV, as defined by the NIOSH, is violence perpetrated by patients or their relatives against healthcare workers. The ED WPV-Q assesses both organizational and environmental risk factors associated with WPV. These factors include the physical design of the ED, such as layout, lighting, and security measures, as well as organizational aspects like worker training, company WPV prevention policies, and patient information protocols. The study employed the Delphi method for validation, involving a panel of experts in occupational risk assessment and safety training. The experts reviewed and rated the questionnaire items using a Likert scale, with a focus on reaching a consensus on the critical elements of the questionnaire. The validation process revealed that the ED WPV-Q effectively calculates a WPV Risk (WPV-R) index, defined as the product of sentinel events (SE) and risk factors (RF). The questionnaire was piloted in an Italian hospital ED, revealing a medium level of WPV risk among both physicians and nurses. The ED WPV-Q proved to be a reliable tool for identifying specific risk factors and guiding targeted organizational and environmental interventions. It offers a strong foundation for developing effective prevention strategies and improving safety in emergency departments. Cronbach's alpha was calculated as 0.90 and showed high internal consistency among the respondents for the items included in the final questionnaire.

Finally, Vrablik et al. [26] developed a multi-level framework for preventing and managing workplace violence in emergency departments, based on data from two U.S. health systems. Using a grounded theory approach, they identified Micro, Meso, and Macro themes aligned with prevention, response, and recovery phases. The model promotes proactive strategies and supports healthcare worker safety and well-being.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review summarizes recent studies on workplace violence management and prevention interventions aimed at healthcare workers, focusing on reducing the number of violent incidents and/or enhancing staff preparedness in managing aggressive behaviors.

The first finding from the literature review highlights the fundamental role of staff education in improving the understanding, skills, and confidence necessary in situations of potential violence. Enhancing the ability of staff to promptly recognize dangerous situations is crucial. This proactive approach enables healthcare workers to act decisively and confidently, preventing escalation and intervening effectively before the situation deteriorates.

The integration of risk assessment tools and comprehensive training programs for healthcare workers is essential in managing workplace violence [27,28]. These measures allow for the early identification of “at risk” patients and reduce unplanned security responses. Therefore, this proactive approach in training enhances staff preparedness and reduces violent incidents in healthcare settings.

The de-escalation technique, identified in the literature as the most widely used method in clinical practice [29,30], emerges as a fundamental tool for reducing risk and supporting the victims of violence. The de-escalation technique consists of a set of verbal and non-verbal strategies used to help patients calm down and cooperate during assessment and treatment, especially in emergency settings [31]. This technique can significantly reduce agitation and the risk of violence in emergency situations [32]. Although attention has been focused on psychopharmacological methods for managing agitated patients, there is a growing recognition of the need to integrate behavioral techniques with pharmacological strategies to provide a comprehensive approach to managing patient aggression.

Another finding from the analysis of the studies included in this review is the importance of interactive training, which appears to be crucial for increasing healthcare workers' confidence in managing aggression, as shown by the studies of Gupta et al. and Brunero et al. [18,19]. The integration of principles from various disciplines, as demonstrated by the study from Liu et al. [20], which combines simulation-based medical education with behavioral economics principles, represents a more holistic and thorough approach to violence prevention. These interdisciplinary strategies are reshaping training paradigms, making them more adaptable to the dynamic nature of healthcare settings.

Recent evidence shows that workplace violence in healthcare is a persistent, multifactorial issue, shaped by organizational, interpersonal, and contextual determinants. Large-scale scoping, systematic, and realist reviews highlight that prevention strategies vary widely in effectiveness and that multilevel, system-oriented approaches, integrating training, organizational policies, and environmental modifications, are more effective than isolated interventions [[33], [34], [35]]. These findings are consistent with the results of the present review, which emphasize the importance of comprehensive, multidisciplinary strategies in reducing violent incidents and improving healthcare worker preparedness.

Somani et al. [36] found that multi-week structured education programs are more effective in addressing workplace violence than standalone sessions, which have limited impact. Their findings highlight the importance of multicomponent interventions that combine training with organizational changes and ongoing support [37]. The studies reviewed emphasize that effective violence prevention requires organizational support, not just individual training. A culture that prioritizes safety through policies, resources, and a supportive environment empowers healthcare workers and enhances their performance. This underscores the need for a multidimensional strategy to ensure safe healthcare settings. In broader occupational contexts, recent research has also highlighted the impact of violence on different worker populations, underscoring the need for tailored prevention approaches across diverse settings [38].

As emerged from the study by Sharifi et al. a considerable percentage of nurses perceived a lack of clear guidelines for reporting violence and an absence of specific training in violence management [21]. This suggests that the introduction of standardized procedures and targeted training can make a significant difference in the perception and management of workplace safety.

Developing and validating tools to assess violence risk in healthcare is essential. These tools help monitor factors such as physical layout, policies, staff training, and patient management, allowing timely updates to safety protocols and addressing both organizational and environmental aspects [25,26]. The American Psychiatric Association Task Force on Psychiatric Emergency Services [39] has recommended that staff receive annual training on managing behavioral emergencies. A previous study by Gillam demonstrated that the effectiveness of training decreases over time, suggesting the need for biennial refresher sessions to maintain skills and operational safety [40]. Ongoing training is essential to ensure healthcare professionals are prepared to manage violence using up-to-date de-escalation and emergency techniques. Including violence prevention in continuing education, supported by organizational commitment and validated tools, is key to creating safe environments. This review highlights the importance of a multifaceted approach to address workplace violence effectively.

sec.title: Practical implications and future research
sec.p: The evidence reviewed highlights the need for healthcare organizations to adopt a multidimensional approach to preventing workplace violence. Several actions can be implemented immediately, including periodic simulation-based training to strengthen de-escalation skills, the use of validated risk assessment tools to identify potentially violent situations early, and the development of clear organizational safety policies supported by structured reporting procedures. These measures require appropriate resource allocation, managerial support, and a culture that prioritizes staff safety. At the same time, future research should further investigate the long-term impact of integrated organizational interventions and explore innovative multidisciplinary strategies that combine behavioral, educational, and environmental components. By grounding prevention efforts in scientific evidence and organizational commitment, healthcare systems can foster safer, more respectful, and sustainable working environments for healthcare workers.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This review presents several limitations that should be acknowledged. First, restricting the search to studies published in the last four years and to English-language publications may have introduced temporal and language selection bias. Second, the substantial heterogeneity across study settings, interventions, and outcome measures prevented the possibility of conducting a quantitative synthesis or meta-analysis, and therefore the findings should be interpreted within the context of a narrative synthesis.

sec.title: Authors’ contributions
sec.p: La Barbiera C. was responsible for data acquisition, analysis, and the writing of the manuscript. Mento C. and De Carlo A. contributed to the conception, design, data acquisition, analysis, and interpretation, and critically revised the manuscript. Lombardo C. contributed to data acquisition and interpretation, and drafted the manuscript. Capozza D. provided guidance on the theoretical aspects of the research and critically revised the manuscript. The manuscript was reviewed for language. All authors approved the final version of the manuscript.

sec.title: Ethical statement
sec.p: Ethical approval was not required for this study, as it is a systematic review of previously published literature and does not involve human participants or personal data.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The author received no specific grant or financial support for the research, authorship, or publication of this article.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Increasing aggression during the COVID-19 lockdowns
Violence against doctors in Iraq during the time of COVID-19

Workplace violence perpetrated by clients of health care: a need for safety and trauma‐informed care
Violence towards emergency nurses: the 2016 Italian national Survey—A cross‐sectional study

element-citation.source: Stress: Concepts, Cognition, Emotion, and Behavior

element-citation.article-title: A systematic review of the literature: workplace violence in the emergency department

element-citation.source: Guidelines on Workplace Violence in the Health Sector

element-citation.article-title: Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being

Prevalence of workplace violence and effects of de-escalation training among hospitalists: a pilot study
